title: Trapping 11,000 Atoms in a Tweezer Array Generated by a Single Metasurface
authors: Yuqing Wang, Zhongchi Zhang, Tao Zhang, Yuxuan Liao, Hanteng Wang, Ye Tian, Binjie Ji, Yujia Wu, Luming Ma, Chen Qing, Chengshu Li, Wei Zhang, Yidong Huang, Wenjun Zhang, Xue Feng, Wenlan Chen, Hui Zhai
abstract: The scalability of physical qubit numbers is a central challenge toward a universal fault-tolerant quantum computer. The inherent scalability of atom array quantum computers stems from the identical nature of atomic qubits, so the available qubit resource is primarily limited by the number of atoms that can be trapped and controlled. Here, we robustly trap 11,000 individual atoms in a tweezer array, thereby enabling the available qubit resource to reach the tens-of-thousands scale for the first time among all quantum computation platforms. This advance is enabled by a single metasurface, approximately 2 cm in diameter, that generates the entire tweezer array without the need for microscope objectives, thereby maximizing laser-power efficiency. The large aperture ensures a working distance of about 1.5 cm, allowing the metasurface to be placed outside the vacuum cell and avoiding the technical complications of in-vacuum operation. We further characterize the randomly loaded atom array using the statistical theory of percolation phase transitions. This work takes an important first step toward a quantum computer at the 10,000-qubit scale.
summary: This paper demonstrates a breakthrough in scaling atom-based quantum computers by trapping 11,000 atoms using a tweezer array generated by a single metasurface. This approach overcomes limitations of traditional optics, enabling the realization of large-scale quantum processors. Furthermore, the authors analyze the array's connectivity using percolation theory, providing both a physical platform and a theoretical characterization of the system's disorder.
main_problem: The central challenge in quantum computing is scaling the number of physical qubits, which is limited by the number of controllable trapped atoms.
main_result: The authors successfully trapped 11,000 individual atoms in a tweezer array generated by a single metasurface, reaching the tens-of-thousands scale for qubit resources.
method: They utilized a single metasurface to generate the entire tweezer array, avoiding complex in-vacuum optics, and characterized the resulting random array using percolation theory.
model_or_system: The system uses neutral atoms (e.g., Rb) trapped in an optical tweezer array generated by a metasurface. The array size is large (18,225 sites), and the collective properties are analyzed using percolation theory.
key_observables: Average trapped atom count ($\sim 11,022$), site-resolved loading probability ($\sim 60\%$), percolation probability $P(p, L)$, and trap depth ($\sim 0.3$ mK).
important_parameters: Metasurface diameter ($\sim 2$ cm), working distance ($\sim 1.5$ cm), and the critical percolation probability ($p_c \approx 0.59$).
main_assumptions: The metasurface is placed outside the vacuum cell to avoid technical complications, and the analysis accounts for propagation through air and glass.
figures_summary: Figures show the experimental setup, raw single-shot fluorescence images confirming $\sim 11,000$ atoms, and plots demonstrating the percolation transition using finite-size scaling.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup using the metasurface, quantifies the trapping of a large number of atoms, and then applies statistical theory (percolation) to characterize the connectivity and disorder within the randomly loaded array.
why_interesting: This work bridges advanced optical engineering (metasurfaces) with fundamental quantum simulation challenges (scaling and disorder), providing a robust platform for studying many-body physics in controllable, large-scale arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental configuration and statistical characterization of the tweezer array. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup. Key components include a Faraday isolator and polarization optics for beam conditioning, two telescope stages (f1–f4) for beam expansion, a reflective spatial filter to truncate the Gaussian tail, the metasurface as the primary diffractive element, and a monitoring system comprising a high-NA objective, tube lens (f5), and camera. (b) Histogram of the normalized peak intensity distribution of individual tweezers. (c) Histogram of the measured Airy disk radius of tweezers in the focal plane.

FIG. 2. Large-scale single-atom trapping performance in the 18,225-site array. (a) Raw single-shot fluorescence image of 87Rb atoms stochastically loaded into the 135×135 metasurface tweezer array. Each bright spot represents a detected single atom; this single shot shows 11400 trapped atoms. (b) Statistical distribution of the filling fraction over 100 consecutive experimental iterations. The system achieves an average loading of 11,022 atoms with a standard deviation of σ = 167, demonstrating high macroscopic stability. (c) Histogram of the site-resolved loading probability across the entire array. The distribution peaks at approximately 60%.

FIG. 3. Percolation transition from atom-loading snapshots. (a) Example configurations. Samples of L × L config- urations are chosen within each experimental snapshot. Filled and open circles denote occupied sites and vacant tweezers, respectively, and solid bonds indicate connected links. The green patch illustrates a percolating configuration (X = 1); the red patch does not percolate (X = 0). (b) Percolation probability P(p, L) as a function of loading fraction p for different patch sizes L. The dashed vertical line marks pc ≈0.59. (c) Finite-size scaling collapse plotted against (p −pc)L1/ν with ν = 4/3.
